food_subgroup: Noodle soup with fried chicken and curry broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken karaage curry udon. FOODON_00002473 (udon noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKN (noodle soup dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for karaage curry udon.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of chicken karaage curry udon, approximately 500 g, comprising udon noodles, curry broth, and 2–3 pieces of fried chicken karaage.

nutrient_source.title: Curry udon with chicken (composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: udon noodles cooked, chicken thigh deep-fried, Japanese curry broth components)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken karaage curry udon as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 45% cooked udon noodles (USDA FDC #169737), 25% deep-fried chicken thigh (USDA FDC #171477), 25% Japanese curry broth (estimated from curry roux and dashi components, USDA FDC #172234 and #169820), and 5% garnish (spring onion). Per-100g values are derived from this weighted blend. Energy cross-checked against NCC Food and Nutrient Database category anchors for Japanese noodle soups.

gi_source.title: Udon noodles, GI = 55 (boiled); analogous estimate applied to curry udon composite dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports boiled udon noodles at GI = 55 (low). Chicken karaage curry udon is a composite dish where udon noodles constitute the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. The curry broth and fried chicken components contribute fat and protein that further attenuate glycemic response. GI of 55 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 62.5) / 100 = 34.4 g per 500 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry broth and soy-based seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 500 g serving provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from udon noodles as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving delivers approximately 62.5 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 34.4, which may require insulin or medication adjustment; consuming a smaller portion or substituting with konjac udon can reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried karaage chicken and sodium from curry broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that the deep-fried karaage component contributes saturated fat and the curry broth contributes significant sodium; limiting frequency of consumption and requesting lighter frying or grilled chicken substitution is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Karaage Curry Udon is a Japanese noodle dish combining thick, chewy udon noodles served in a rich Japanese curry-flavoured dashi broth, topped with pieces of karaage — Japanese-style deep-fried chicken marinated in soy sauce, ginger, and garlic, then coated in potato starch and fried until golden and crispy. The dish offers a contrast of textures: the soft, slippery udon noodles against the crunchy exterior of the karaage chicken. The curry broth is typically made from a blend of dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and Japanese curry roux, producing a mildly spiced, umami-rich, slightly sweet and savoury soup. Garnishes commonly include sliced spring onion and occasionally a soft-boiled egg or tempura flakes. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 590 kcal per 500 g serving), provides meaningful protein from the fried chicken (approximately 36 g per serving), and delivers substantial carbohydrate from the udon noodles (approximately 62.5 g per serving). Sodium content is high due to the curry broth and soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), anchored to boiled udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate to high at approximately 34.4. Clinically, this dish warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or soy allergies. In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese casual dining restaurants such as GyoGyo at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken karaage curry udon as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked udon noodles, deep-fried chicken thigh, and Japanese curry broth components. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported and the assembly methodology is documented.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. All other micronutrients are estimated from the weighted component assembly. The food_name has been normalised from the input '604D Chicken Kaarage Curry Udon(D)' to the canonical English form 'Chicken Karaage Curry Udon'; the original menu designation '604D' is a restaurant item code retained in aliases. The spelling 'Kaarage' in the original input is a variant romanisation of the Japanese '唐揚げ' (karaage); both spellings are captured in aliases. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual restaurant portion may vary. Sodium content is high and is the primary clinical concern for this dish. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for boiled udon noodles; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component assembly and carry moderate uncertainty.
